136	# jjezs_pace ₢A_AAJ
137	
138	## Character
139	
140	Research completed — decisions recorded. Pace is **railed last** in ₣A_ by deliberate plan: when its turn comes (after AAS through AAM all wrap), reslate this pace from research into a **heat-construction directive** that synthesizes the inventory below into a pace decomposition, then nominates the moorings rename heat. AAJ's wrap closes ₣A_ and the rename heat exists as a freshly-nominated sibling.
141	
142	This deferred-reslate pattern keeps the research finding tied to the heat that surfaced it (no orphan future-heat drift) without forcing the heat-sized rename into ₣A_'s scope (which is GCP/cloud-side path grammar, orthogonal from filesystem moorings).
143	
144	## Decision
145	
146	### Principle
147	
148	**Visibility aligns with authorship.** Consumer-authored content is visible at top level; kit machinery is hidden or lives under `Tools/`. Dot-prefix on consumer-authored directories (`.rbk/`, `.buk/`) contradicts the handbook's explicit teaching that learners should read those files. Visibility signals importance to the human reader ([Lobsters — "Use `.config` to store your project configs"](https://lobste.rs/s/wac58n/use_config_store_your_project_configs)).
149	
150	### Renames
151	
152	| Current | Becomes | Notes |
153	|---------|---------|-------|
154	| `.rbk/` + `.buk/` | `rbmm_moorings/` | Unified consumer configuration umbrella |
155	| `.buk/launcher.*.sh` | `rbmm_moorings/rbml_launchers/` | All launchers merged, no kit segregation |
156	| `.buk/users/` | `rbmm_moorings/rbmu_users/` | Remote device profile registry |
157	| `rbev-vessels/` | `rbmm_moorings/rbmv_vessels/` | Default for `RBRR_VESSEL_DIR`; customer can override |
158	| `.rbk/rbob_compose.yml` | `Tools/rbk/rbob_compose.yml` | Kit machinery (crucible topology), not consumer tunable |
159	| `.buk/rbbc_constants.sh` | Eliminated | No longer needed — layout is standardized, kit-side constants suffice |
160	| `Tools/rbk/rbmP_laterSecurityAudit.md` | `Memos/` | Orphan memo; `rbm*` namespace reclaimed for moorings family |
161	
162	### Prefix family: `rbm*_`
163	
164	Gestalt: user-facing content inside moorings. A new concept-prefix family under RB.
165	
166	| Prefix | Meaning | Location |
167	|--------|---------|----------|
168	| `rbmm_` | Moorings umbrella (the directory itself) | `rbmm_moorings/` |
169	| `rbml_` | Moorings launchers | `rbmm_moorings/rbml_launchers/` |
170	| `rbmu_` | Moorings users (remote profiles) | `rbmm_moorings/rbmu_users/` |
171	| `rbmv_` | Moorings vessels | `rbmm_moorings/rbmv_vessels/` |
172	
173	`rbm` is non-terminal. Future `rbm?_` slots remain open for other user-facing moorings concerns.
174	
175	### Final layout
176	
177	```
178	rbmm_moorings/                      <- single consumer-owned entry at repo root
179	  burc.env                          <- BUK regime (authored)
180	  rbrr.env                          <- RBK repo regime (authored)
181	  rbrp.env                          <- RBK payor regime (authored)
182	  tadmor/
183	    rbrn.env                        <- nameplate regime (authored)
184	    compose.yml                     <- per-nameplate overlay (authored)
185	  ccyolo/ ...
186	  pluml/ ...
187	  srjcl/ ...
188	  rbml_launchers/
189	    launcher.rbw_workbench.sh
190	    launcher_nolog.rbw_workbench.sh
191	    launcher.jjw_workbench.sh
192	    launcher.buw_workbench.sh
193	    launcher.cmw_workbench.sh
194	    launcher.study_workbench.sh
195	    launcher.vow_workbench.sh
196	    launcher.vvw_workbench.sh
197	    launcher.vslw_workbench.sh
198	    launcher.apcw_workbench.sh
199	    launcher.rbtw_workbench.sh
200	  rbmu_users/
201	    <BURH-style profiles>
202	  rbmv_vessels/
203	    common-sentry-context/
204	    rbev-bottle-ifrit/              <- kit-shipped, keeps rbev- provenance marker
205	    rbev-bottle-plantuml/ ...
206	    <user vessels without rbev- prefix>
207	Tools/
208	  rbk/
209	    rbob_compose.yml                <- base crucible topology (kit-shipped)
210	    rbob_bottle.sh
211	    ...
212	  buk/ ...
213	tt/
214	  z-launcher.sh                     <- trampoline, hardcodes ../rbmm_moorings/rbml_launchers/
215	  rbw-*.sh ...                      <- tabtargets route through z-launcher
216	```
217	
218	### Trampoline design
219	
220	Every tabtarget currently hardcodes `.buk/launcher_*_workbench.sh` — the single most pervasive path reference in the system. Introducing `tt/z-launcher.sh` as a stable indirection means future moorings-directory renames touch exactly one file. Tabtargets call the trampoline with a workbench identifier; the trampoline resolves `../rbmm_moorings/rbml_launchers/launcher.{id}.sh` (or equivalent).
221	
222	The `rbmm_moorings/` path inside `z-launcher.sh` is hardcoded. Customers adjust it at project start if they choose to rename the moorings directory. That's a deliberate single-point-of-customization.
223	
224	### Rejected / decided alternatives
225	
226	- **Kit subdirectories inside moorings** (`rbmm_moorings/rbk/`, `rbmm_moorings/buk/`) — rejected. File prefixes (`burc.`, `rbrr.`, `rbrn.`) already encode kit; subdirs would be redundant nesting, and the kit boundary is a producer concern not a consumer one.
227	- **Separate `regime/` and `vessels/` top-level dirs** — rejected. Single `rbmm_moorings/` umbrella is cleaner. Vessels are consumer-configured (even if rarely edited), belong with the rest of the configuration surface.
228	- **`rbmb_` bootstrap-constants module** — rejected. Original `rbbc_constants.sh` existed to single-source the `.rbk` literal across many files. With `rbmm_moorings/` as the standardized, z-launcher-hardcoded name, no cross-file indirection is needed.
229	- **Bridge period (both old and new paths work transiently)** — rejected. Hard cutover is cleaner.
230	- **Extend moorings to JJK/CMK/VVK** — rejected. `rbmm_moorings/` is RBK+BUK only. JJK uses `.claude/jjm/` for agent-side state (different kettle). Other kits keep their own conventions.
231	- **Visible `config/` or `regime/`** — rejected. `config/` throws away the project's distinctive vocabulary; `regime/` is too narrow (regimes are formal configurations; moorings also contains bootstrap glue and vessels).
232	
233	### Prefix coexistence note
234	
235	Inside `rbmm_moorings/`, regime files retain their original family prefixes (`burc_`, `rbrr_`, `rbrn_`, `rbrp_`). The `rbm*_` family is the moorings-infrastructure prefix — directories and any new moorings-level code. This is a feature, not a conflict: regime file prefixes tell you which kit's regime you're reading; `rbm*_` tells you it's moorings infrastructure.
236	
237	## Migration scope (for the follow-up rename heat)
238	
239	The actual filesystem rename is out of scope for this heat (₣A_). It is intended as a separate follow-up heat. The migration scope below is captured here so the follow-up heat has a starting inventory.
240	
241	### Code references
242	
243	- ~33 files in `Tools/` reference paths starting with `.rbk/`, `.buk/`, or `rbev-vessels/`
244	- Every tabtarget in `tt/` hardcodes `.buk/launcher...` — mechanical sed sweep, then tabtargets route through `z-launcher.sh` going forward
245	- `BURD_LAUNCHER` value in each tabtarget → replaced with workbench identifier passed to trampoline
246	- `RBRR_VESSEL_DIR` default flips to `rbmm_moorings/rbmv_vessels/`
247	
248	### Handbook and specs
249	
250	- Every handbook track that teaches `.rbk/`, `.buk/`, `rbev-vessels/` — update vocabulary and examples
251	- Specs: RBS0, RBRN, RBSRR, RBSRP, RBSRV, and others that reference these paths
252	- README.md, CLAUDE.md, consumer CLAUDE.md (`Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/CLAUDE.consumer.md`)
253	- AsciiDoc linked terms that reference the old dir names
254	
255	### BCG codification
256	
257	The trampoline (`tt/z-launcher.sh`) is a **new pattern** — single-point-of-customization for moorings location at the tabtarget layer, parallel to BURC providing it at the CLI layer (BCG:171-181 dispatch-provided `BURD_*`). Without BCG codification it remains tribal knowledge.
258	
259	Add a subsection peer to "Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables" titled **"Tabtarget Path Indirection"** stating:
260	
261	> Tabtargets must not hardcode moorings/config-directory paths. The single tabtarget `tt/z-launcher.sh` resolves the moorings location and dispatches to the named launcher; all other tabtargets invoke it with a workbench identifier. This makes the moorings location a single-point-of-customization at the tabtarget layer, parallel to BURC providing it at the CLI layer.
262	
263	The BCG update lands in the rename heat **alongside** the trampoline introduction, not before — BCG should not document vapor. The pattern also extends BCG's CLI-as-Module-Gateway principle (BCG:44-77) one level further: tabtargets delegate path resolution through a stable indirection layer rather than hardcoding implementation details.
264	
265	### Infrastructure
266	
267	- Marshal zero template (currently creates `.rbk/` and `.buk/` for new consumers) — regenerate to create `rbmm_moorings/` layout
268	- Test fixtures that create these directories
269	- Any diagrams showing the directory structure
270	
271	### Cleanup
272	
273	- Delete `rbbc_constants.sh` (absorbed into kit code)
274	- Move `rbmP_laterSecurityAudit.md` -> `Memos/`
275	- Move `rbob_compose.yml` from `.rbk/` to `Tools/rbk/`
276	- Register new `rbm*_` prefixes in CLAUDE.md File Acronym Mappings
277	
278	### Observed non-issue
279	
280	**gitignore patterns** — nothing in `.rbk/`, `.buk/`, or `rbev-vessels/` is currently gitignored. Migration doesn't touch ignore files.
281	
282	## Out of scope
283	
284	- Actually executing the renames (separate follow-up heat, not a pace in this heat)
285	- Extending moorings pattern to other kits
286	- Eliminating per-nameplate overlay `compose.yml` files (they ARE consumer-authored)
287	- Redesigning the launcher infrastructure beyond introducing `z-launcher.sh`
288	
289	## Paddock update on wrap
290	
291	A_ paddock should record (additive to what's already there):
292	- The `rbm*_` prefix family and its gestalt ("user-facing content inside moorings")
293	- The hard-cutover discipline for the moorings rename (when its own heat eventually runs)
294	- The BCG-codification commitment: rename heat lands trampoline AND adds BCG "Tabtarget Path Indirection" subsection
295	
296	The filesystem rename is NOT a prerequisite to this heat's ₢A_AAE — it's a separate follow-up heat. Paddock should not claim otherwise.

308	## Character
309	
310	Mechanical — small-scope extraction of OCI Distribution Spec protocol keywords (`manifests`, `blobs`, `tags/list`, `v2`) that currently appear as bare literals in URL construction across rbfr and rbfv. Low per-site judgment once the naming scheme is chosen; the design thought lies in choosing between named-constant replacement vs a URL-builder helper.
311	
312	## Docket
313	
314	The OCI Registry HTTP API v2 URL shape `/v2/<name>/manifests/<reference>` (and peers `blobs/`, `tags/list`) appears ~10× across `rbfr_FoundryRetriever.sh` and `rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh` with the endpoint keywords inlined as bare string literals. These are protocol-defined keywords from the OCI Distribution Spec — frozen, not arbitrary — but repeated inlining violates BCG's Load-Bearing Complexity principle. See BCG:609 `Z«PREFIX»_API_VERSION="v2"` as the canonical pattern for capturing protocol keywords.
315	
316	### Two options (pick one at pace execution)
317	
318	**Option A — named protocol constants as RBFC tinder**. Add `ZRBFC_ENDPOINT_MANIFESTS="manifests"`, `ZRBFC_ENDPOINT_BLOBS="blobs"`, `ZRBFC_ENDPOINT_TAGS_LIST="tags/list"`, `ZRBFC_API_VERSION="v2"` at RBFC tinder-constant position (post-`SOURCED`, pre-kindle). Each site replaces the bare literal with the named constant. Minimal structural change, most direct expression of BCG's tinder-constant pattern.
319	
320	**Option B — URL-builder helper**. Add `zrbfc_manifest_url_capture <vessel_path> <reference>` returning `${ZRBFC_REGISTRY_API_BASE}/<vessel_path>/manifests/<reference>`. Callers collapse to one line. Higher upfront effort, higher payoff if future OCI endpoints get added. Tilts toward "one place owns the URL shape."

## Paddock Summary

The heat is three interwoven infrastructure cleanups culminating in a depot regeneration. **Thread 1 — Resource prefixing** (paces AAA/AAB/AAC, all closed) introduces `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` for cloud-visible names (GAR, Cloud Build, GCP) and `RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX` for local container/network names, enabling multi-regime isolation on shared infrastructure. **Thread 2 — GAR categorical layout migration** (AAK/AAL, closed) reorganizes the GAR top-level namespace into three prefixed categories (`<prefix>hallmarks/<hallmark>/{image,vouch,pouch,about,attest,diags}`, `<prefix>reliquaries/<date>/<tool>`, `<prefix>enshrines/<base>`), dissolves the `<vessel>:<hallmark>-<suffix>` hyphen-suffix scheme and the `vouches/` aggregator, and simplifies tabtargets from `(vessel, hallmark)` to `(hallmark)`. **Thread 3 — Payor credential subdirectory** (AAD, closed) moves `rbro.env` into a `payor/` subdirectory to match the role-subdirectory pattern already established for RBRA credentials.

The depot regen at the tail (`₢A_AAE`) is the **hard cutover point** — all three threads land before it, and depot regen validates the new infrastructure end-to-end against fresh GCP infrastructure. Discipline is **hard cutover** — old and new path shapes must not coexist in live code. Vessel identity no longer lives in the GAR path; it's resolved on demand from `vouch` or `about` metadata. `RBGC_ARK_SUFFIX_*` constants persist but rename to `RBGC_ARK_BASENAME_*` (values lose the leading hyphen, role shifts from "suffix-to-hyphen-concat" to "literal sibling-file basename"). Attest and vouch persist as peer basenames — the split is load-bearing (attest advertises during build, vouch checks at consumption).

A separate-but-related research result captured under `₢A_AAJ`: the `rbm*_` moorings prefix family and the `rbmm_moorings/` umbrella for consumer-authored content — but the actual filesystem rename **flows to a separate follow-up heat**, NOT a pace in this heat. AAJ wraps by reslating from research into a heat-construction directive; AAJ's wrap closes ₣A_.

### 2. ₢A_AAU — `cloudbuild-ark-basename-passthrough` [rough]

Mechanical extraction across a producer-consumer language boundary. GAR ark basenames (`image`, `vouch`, `pouch`, `about`, `attest`, `diags`) currently appear as bare literals inside Cloud Build step scripts — the Director-side `RBGC_ARK_BASENAME_*` constants don't propagate across the CB submission boundary. A future Director-side rename would silently mismatch CB-side literals; producer/consumer would disagree on paths. Pattern: pass-through matching the existing `_RBG?_HALLMARKS_ROOT` discipline; add `_RBG?_ARK_BASENAME_*` entries to the three groups (`_RBGY_*`, `_RBGA_*`, `_RBGV_*`) that need them. Success: fast suite 75/75 + crucible suite green; one conjure ordain against `rbev-busybox` lands all 5-6 ark packages; one graft ordain against `rbev-graft-demo` succeeds.

## Thoughts on splitting AAS to `../rbm_beta_recipemuster`

I'd push back on splitting **AAS specifically**. Three reasons:

1. **AAS consumes a singleton: live GAR + Cloud Build.** The "split to a parallel tree" pattern works for code-level parallelism. AAS exercises live infrastructure (hallmark `c260425094751-r260425164754` is currently live in GAR, awaiting abjure). Two trees can't run AAS-style live-infra work simultaneously without fighting over the same depot — the parallel tree gives you nothing here.

2. **AAS is sequential by docket.** Its Cadence section explicitly mandates a 1→4 ordering (verify gap → re-run SingleCases → original docket items in order). It's not internally parallelizable.

3. **AAS already has 8 commits on main.** Splitting it now means either branching from current `main` (which is just a regular branch, no separate-directory benefit) or trying to extract the 8 commits to a side branch — that's churn for no gain.

**Better candidate: AAN.** It's literally designed for parallel-officium execution — the docket has an explicit blacklist of AAS-affiliated theurge files baked in. All design decisions are pre-made (mechanical apply). One notch at the end. No GCP dependencies for the core work (Tier 2/3 verification touches Payor install but read-only / interactive). Running AAN in `../rbm_beta_recipemuster` while you finish AAS in `../rbm_alpha_recipemuster` is the use case the docket was written for.

**Other strong parallel-tree candidates**: AAM (pure OCI string extraction, explicitly independent), AAY (BCG-spec study, no GCP). AAU touches CB step scripts that AAS doesn't, but AAU was deliberately railed *after* AAS to keep theurge-catchup uninterrupted — a parallel tree changes that calculus.

(Note: the paddock has no `## Character` section — it leads with `## Context`, then `## Design decisions captured`, then `## References`.)

The heat is three interwoven infrastructure cleanups culminating in a depot regeneration. **Thread 1 — Resource prefixing** (₢A_AAA/AAB/AAC, all closed) introduces `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` (cloud-visible names: GAR, Cloud Build, GCP) and `RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX` (local container/network names) to enable multi-regime isolation on shared infrastructure. **Thread 2 — GAR categorical layout migration** (₢A_AAK/AAL, closed) reorganizes the GAR top-level namespace into three prefixed categories (`<prefix>hallmarks/<hallmark>/{image,vouch,pouch,about,attest,diags}`, `<prefix>reliquaries/<date>/<tool>`, `<prefix>enshrines/<base>`), dissolves the `<vessel>:<hallmark>-<suffix>` hyphen-suffix scheme, removes the `vouches/` aggregator, and simplifies tabtargets from `(vessel, hallmark)` to `(hallmark)`. **Thread 3 — Payor credential subdirectory** (₢A_AAD, closed) moves `rbro.env` into a `payor/` subdirectory matching the role-subdirectory pattern. The depot regen at the tail (₢A_AAE) is the **hard cutover point** — all three threads must land before it; the regen validates the new infrastructure end-to-end against fresh GCP infrastructure. Key constraints: the `RBGC_ARK_SUFFIX_*` constants are renamed to `RBGC_ARK_BASENAME_*` (values drop the leading hyphen — concat grammar disappears entirely from ark path construction); attest/vouch are kept as load-bearing peer basenames; vessel identity no longer lives in GAR paths and is resolved on demand from `vouch`/`about` metadata; pouch is present for conjure/bind, absent for graft. ### Parallelism vs sequential

The docket is **sequential by design**. Cadence step 2 ("close the verification gap") is a single-track ~10–12-minute live-infra run that must be GREEN before steps 3+. The original AAS scope (steps 1–4 inside the docket) is also "in declared dependency order." Within Step 1 (fast-suite negatives) the three Rust test cases are file-independent edits to `rbtdrf_fast.rs` and could in principle parallelize, but the docket frames the work as a single mechanical sweep and requires sequential test execution per repo discipline ("Sequential only — fixtures share regime state and container namespaces"). Step 2's cases (`summon`, `plumb_full`, `plumb_compact`, `rekon`, `jettison`) involve placement-decision judgment that pulls toward sequential. Recommend **single-track sonnet/opus, sequential** — file boundaries don't earn parallelism here, and live-infra verification gates must close before additive work resumes.

# Heat Summary: ₣A_ — rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen

## Heat overview

- **Silks**: `rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen`
- **Firemark**: `₣A_`
- **Status**: `racing`
- **Created**: 260415
- **Total paces**: 26 (12 completed with commits, 14 remaining)
- **Remaining**: 14 paces, all currently `[rough]`
- **Completed (per file-touch bitmap)**: paces 1–11 closed; pace 12 (S = `theurge-coverage-catchup-aak-aal-aao` / ₢A_AAS) has 8 commits but is **still open** and is the first remaining pace.

## Paddock summary

(Note: the paddock has no `## Character` section — it leads with `## Context`, then `## Design decisions captured`, then `## References`.)

The heat is three interwoven infrastructure cleanups culminating in a depot regeneration. **Thread 1 — Resource prefixing** (₢A_AAA/AAB/AAC, all closed) introduces `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` (cloud-visible names: GAR, Cloud Build, GCP) and `RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX` (local container/network names) to enable multi-regime isolation on shared infrastructure. **Thread 2 — GAR categorical layout migration** (₢A_AAK/AAL, closed) reorganizes the GAR top-level namespace into three prefixed categories (`<prefix>hallmarks/<hallmark>/{image,vouch,pouch,about,attest,diags}`, `<prefix>reliquaries/<date>/<tool>`, `<prefix>enshrines/<base>`), dissolves the `<vessel>:<hallmark>-<suffix>` hyphen-suffix scheme, removes the `vouches/` aggregator, and simplifies tabtargets from `(vessel, hallmark)` to `(hallmark)`. **Thread 3 — Payor credential subdirectory** (₢A_AAD, closed) moves `rbro.env` into a `payor/` subdirectory matching the role-subdirectory pattern. The depot regen at the tail (₢A_AAE) is the **hard cutover point** — all three threads must land before it; the regen validates the new infrastructure end-to-end against fresh GCP infrastructure. Key constraints: the `RBGC_ARK_SUFFIX_*` constants are renamed to `RBGC_ARK_BASENAME_*` (values drop the leading hyphen — concat grammar disappears entirely from ark path construction); attest/vouch are kept as load-bearing peer basenames; vessel identity no longer lives in GAR paths and is resolved on demand from `vouch`/`about` metadata; pouch is present for conjure/bind, absent for graft. Filesystem-side moorings rename (`rbmm_moorings/`, `rbm*_` prefix family — captured in ₢A_AAJ research) is **explicitly out of scope** for this heat — flows to a separate follow-up heat.

## Remaining paces in order

The `jjx_show` output lists remaining paces in this order:

### 1. ₢A_AAS — `theurge-coverage-catchup-aak-aal-aao` *(FIRST REMAINING)*

**Verification-driven completion** of an in-flight pace that has accreted six notches. Originally an additive-coverage pace; today's session pivoted into AAK-completion fixing. Two recent fixes (HTTP 400 + plumb multi-layer) are verified GREEN against live infrastructure but the full SingleCase lifecycle isn't end-to-end clean yet. Pick up at "verify what's already fixed before extending coverage." Mechanical until original docket items are reached, then back to additive shape.

**Six prior notches**: `b602b7c5` AAK preflight + inscribe submitter, `14242011` enshrine sweep, `160ceec7` yoke 6 vessels to reliquary `r260425082412`, `73f3f818` bind AAK-shape + graft platform-normalization, `98b80250` conjure HTTP 400 fix + SingleCase non-crucible context, `23a93bb0` plumb multi-layer extraction.

**Verification gap**: the SingleCase failed at `plumb_full` (now fixed) before reaching `06-rekon` and `07-abjure`. Hallmark `c260425094751-r260425164754` remains live in GAR. **Two paths to close** (recommended path A): re-run `tt/rbtd-s.SingleCase.four-mode.sh rbtdrc_fourmode_conjure_lifecycle` (~10–12 min, full lifecycle from fresh ordain — also re-confirms HTTP 400 + plumb fixes through the case wrapper); OR (path B) run `tt/rbw-ir <hallmark>` + `tt/rbw-fA --force <hallmark>` manually (~1 min, but doesn't exercise SingleCase wrapper). Then re-run bind + graft + kludge SingleCases.

**Then the original AAS docket** (sequential): (1) Fast-suite negatives + smoke in `rbtdrf_fast.rs` (`rv_rbrr_bad_cloud_prefix`, `rv_rbrr_bad_runtime_prefix`, `rs_rbrr_nonempty_prefix`); (2) Four-mode new cases in `RBTDRC_SECTIONS_FOUR_MODE.cases` (`summon`, `plumb_full` decision, `plumb_compact`, `rekon` decision, `jettison`); (3) Batch-vouch standalone fixture `rbtdrc_batch_vouch` (5 cases); (4) Manifest updates in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`; (5) Yoke deferred to AAP.

Four tiers: **Tier 1** automated suites (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.service.sh` 80 cases + `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.crucible.sh` 117 cases, sequential); **Tier 2** hand-run producer paths (inscribe via `rbw-dI`, conjure busybox via `rbw-fO rbev-vessels/rbev-busybox`, enshrine if applicable, graft via `rbw-fO rbev-graft-demo`); **Tier 3** hand-run consumer paths (tally, plumb compact + full, summon, charge tadmor, quench tadmor); **Tier 4** abjure (fresh-conjured + pre-AAK hallmark). **Mechanical apply** — all design decisions baked into the docket. ### Parallelism vs sequential

The docket is **sequential by design**. Cadence step 2 ("close the verification gap") is a single-track ~10–12-minute live-infra run that must be GREEN before steps 3+. The original AAS scope (steps 1–4 inside the docket) is also "in declared dependency order." Step 2's cases (`summon`, `plumb_full`, `plumb_compact`, `rekon`, `jettison`) involve placement-decision judgment that pulls toward sequential. Recommend **single-track sonnet/opus, sequential** — file boundaries don't earn parallelism here, and live-infra verification gates must close before additive work resumes.

message.content: Did the view of the heat presented by 'groom' to you also show completed paces?  I was surprised the output was 86K, requiring you to do a segmented read.

content.text:
No — closed paces did NOT have full dockets in the output. `remaining: true` did its job. Closed paces appeared only as a compact swim-lane bitmap (one row each, file-touch affiliation visualization), no docket bodies.

The 86KB came from the **14 remaining paces** themselves. Several of them carry very long pre-baked dockets, since the heat's discipline is "all design decisions captured at slate time, mechanical apply at execution":

- AAS — verification-driven, lots of forensic context (build IDs, hallmark IDs, six-notch history, two-path verification options)
- AAN — full per-finding F1–F13 mechanical apply spec with file lists, line numbers, verbatim search-replace
- AAP — four-tier burn-in protocol with triage classification rules
- AAV — 11-tabtarget colophon table with rename map + new-mint rationale
- AAW — three-option design question + cost analysis + mode coverage matrix
- AAJ — full research synthesis with prefix family + rename inventory + BCG codification commitment

Average ~100 lines each × 14 = ~1,400 lines, which matches the 1,444 reported.

This is downstream of your "Character + verbose docket" working style — the dockets carry the thinking forward across chats so a fresh agent can mount cold. Tradeoff is exactly what you saw: groom output blows past the inline-tool-result threshold once a heat has more than ~10 detailed remaining paces.

A couple of options if this becomes a recurring annoyance:
1. **`detail: false`** for the initial groom pass (overview + bitmap only), then `jjx_brief <coronet>` for individual paces of interest. Loses the holistic read but keeps groom snappy.
2. **`rough: true` on `jjx_coronets`** if you just want the punch list of remaining paces without dockets.
3. Accept the segmented read for genuine groom sessions — the agent-summarize pattern worked, and the cost is one extra subagent call.

The 86KB isn't pathological for this heat — it reflects deliberate density.

message.content: I'm inclined to furlough a new heat and restring several of the later paces to that heat where we can debug in parallel the 'local' changes such as imageops with the long-running testing against the depot.  Thoughts?

content.text:
The shape is right, but a few decisions worth pinning down before doing it:

**Natural clusters in the remaining paces:**

| Cluster | Paces | Depot-bound? |
|---------|-------|--------------|
| Depot/live-infra spine | AAS → AAU → AAP → **AAE** → AAF → AAG → AAZ | yes (AAE is the cutover) |
| OAuth/BCG cleanup | AAN, AAY | no |
| OCI strings | AAM | no |
| Imageops sub-trio | AAV → AAW → AAX | AAV/AAW local, **AAX hits live GAR** |
| Closer | AAJ | no, but wraps ₣A_ |

**My read:**

- **AAV + AAW are the strongest restring candidates.** Pure local code, internally sequenced, and the imageops theme is coherent enough to name a heat after. AAX is gated by AAV but does cost Cloud Build minutes — it can live in the new heat and just race after AAE settles.
- **AAN + AAY + AAM are also movable but a different theme.** Bundling them with imageops dilutes the new heat's character; leaving them in ₣A_ keeps light mechanical work available between AAS/AAP runs. Or they could become a third heat ("hygiene sweep") — but three heats feels like overhead.
- **AAJ should stay** — it explicitly wraps ₣A_ and nominates the moorings rename. Moving it breaks that contract.

**Two options worth considering:**

1. **Conservative split** — new heat is just `imageops-broaden-precheck-fixtures` (AAV, AAW, AAX). ₣A_ keeps AAS/AAU/AAP/AAE/AAF/AAG/AAZ + AAN/AAY/AAM as cleanup filler + AAJ closer. Clearest theme, smallest blast radius.

2. **Aggressive split** — new heat takes AAN, AAY, AAM, AAV, AAW, AAX. Themed as "post-AAK cleanups parallel to depot regen." ₣A_ shrinks to the live-infra spine. More parallel surface area but the new heat's character becomes "everything that wasn't depot-bound" — less coherent.

I'd lean **option 1**.

**On "furlough":** if you intend to start working the new heat immediately in `../rbm_beta_recipemuster`, you don't need to furlough — just nominate as racing. Furlough makes sense if you want it nominated-but-paused as a backlog placeholder. Which did you mean?

Also worth flagging: AAV's docket calls out a wide spec/prose churn (RBSIR/RBSIJ/RBSIW updates + RBSIM mint, handbook tracks, memos, `rbk-claude-tabtarget-context.md`, four-mode test cases). Doing AAV in a parallel tree is fine, but the merge back to main will touch ~20 files that AAS/AAP may also touch in the meantime. Worth a quick file-overlap check before splitting.

message.content: Thank you, yes: nominate a new heat with silks a variant of current and do the aggressive split option now.

content.text: New heat ₣BA created. Now restringing the six coronets.

Heat ₣BA was created and the transfer was drafted, but the commit hit the size guard:

- **Staged content**: 205,055 bytes
- **Limit**: 100,000 bytes
- **Per-file breakdown**: 205,055 bytes — all in `.claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json` (the 6 paces' full dockets are dense)

Per the discipline I must not auto-override.

**Drafted (not yet committed) coronets in ₣BA:**

| Old | New | Silks |
|-----|-----|-------|
| ₢A_AAN | ₢BAAAA | oauth-trust-hygiene-sweep |
| ₢A_AAY | ₢BAAAB | bcg-shellcheck-cross-module-discipline |
| ₢A_AAM | ₢BAAAC | oci-magic-string-extraction |
| ₢A_AAV | ₢BAAAD | imageops-broaden |
| ₢A_AAW | ₢BAAAE | imageops-precheck |
| ₢A_AAX | ₢BAAAF | imageops-fixtures |

(Renumbering — note the imageops sub-trio AAV→AAW→AAX preserves order as BAAAD→BAAAE→BAAAF, but AAN/AAY/AAM come first in BA. If you want imageops first in the new heat, we'd reorder after.)

**Two ways forward:**

1. **Raise the limit** for this single transfer (e.g., `size_limit: 250000`). One commit, atomic. The 205KB is one-time — gallops state moves once and doesn't replay.

2. **Split into batches** — e.g., transfer the three imageops paces first, then the three cleanup paces. Two commits, each under the limit.

I lean toward option 1 — the size is structural (6 mechanical-apply dockets), splitting just makes more commits without reducing total churn. But your call.

```markdown
# Paddock: rbk-mvp-3-cleanups-and-imageops

## Context

Sibling heat to ₣A_ (rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen). Holds six post-AAK paces that don't depend on AAE depot regen, so they can race in parallel to ₣A_'s live-infra spine — intended to execute in `../rbm_beta_recipemuster` while ₣A_ continues on the primary tree.

## Themes

| Coronet | Origin | Theme |
|---------|--------|-------|
| ₢BAAAA | ex-A_AAN | oauth-trust-hygiene-sweep |
| ₢BAAAB | ex-A_AAY | bcg-shellcheck-cross-module-discipline |
| ₢BAAAC | ex-A_AAM | oci-magic-string-extraction |
| ₢BAAAD | ex-A_AAV | imageops-broaden |
| ₢BAAAE | ex-A_AAW | imageops-precheck |
| ₢BAAAF | ex-A_AAX | imageops-fixtures |

Three sub-clusters: hygiene sweep (BAAAA/BAAAB), OCI string extraction (BAAAC), imageops sub-trio (BAAAD → BAAAE → BAAAF). Each pace's docket carries full design intent — paddock stays light by deliberate choice.

## Cross-officium discipline

Hard file blacklists persist across the tree split — file boundaries enforce coordination, not directory boundaries. ₢BAAAA's blacklist of ₢A_AAS theurge files (`rbtdrc_crucible.rs`, `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`, `rbtdrf_fast.rs`, etc.) is the most prominent. Crucible/complete suite runs are reserved for ₣A_'s ₢A_AAP burn-in; this heat runs fast suite only during the parallel period.

## Coordination with ₣A_

- ₢BAAAA's per-file SC2153 directives become redundant once ₢BAAAB lands.
- ₢BAAAC (OCI strings) is fully independent of AAE/AAK/AAL.
- ₢BAAAD spec/prose churn (~20 files: RBSIR/RBSIJ/RBSIW + RBSIM, handbook tracks, memos, `rbk-claude-tabtarget-context.md`) may overlap with ₣A_'s ₢A_AAP burn-in fixes — file-level discipline, merge cleanly when each pace wraps.
- ₢BAAAF is the only pace touching live GCP (Cloud Build minutes for service-tier fixtures); coordinate against ₣A_'s spend if concurrent.

## References

- Parent heat ₣A_ paddock — design history for resource prefixing, GAR categorical layout, payor subdir migration
- BCG `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md` (referenced by ₢BAAAB)
```

message.content: Paddocks should not include details about paces and their ordering.  The order of the paces carries that.  Paddocks are for end to end context needed for the paces in the heat.  Reconsider.

content.text:
You're right — I was enumerating paces, which is the rail's job. Revised:

```markdown
# Paddock: rbk-mvp-3-cleanups-and-imageops

## Context

Sibling heat to ₣A_ (rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen). Holds the post-AAK paces whose work is independent of AAE depot regeneration, so they can race in parallel to ₣A_'s live-infra spine. Intended execution pattern: a second officium working in `../rbm_beta_recipemuster` while the primary officium continues ₣A_ in the main tree.

All design decisions are baked into individual dockets — agents executing here should expect mechanical-apply work with explicit file lists and verbatim verification gates.

## Cross-officium discipline

This heat shares the repo with ₣A_; the parallel-tree split is geometric, not semantic. File-level boundaries enforce coordination — a pace's docket may carry an explicit blacklist of files belonging to ₣A_'s active paces, and those constraints persist across directories. When in doubt, additive only: no destructive git operations, no fixing "wrong" repo state without asking, commits land only the file lists each docket specifies.

## Test-suite reservation

During the parallel period, this heat runs fast suite only. Crucible, service, and complete suites are reserved for ₣A_'s live-infra paces — they share regime state and container/network namespaces, and concurrent runs will fail. Once ₣A_'s burn-in clears (or this heat's tail crosses with ₣A_'s wrap), the restriction can lift.

## Live-GCP coordination

Most paces in this heat are pure local refactor or spec churn — no GCP cost. Where a docket does call for live infrastructure, it carries explicit cost notes. Coordinate any billable runs against ₣A_'s active spend; ₣A_ owns the heavier live-infra workload during the parallel period.

## References

- Parent heat ₣A_ paddock — design history for resource prefixing (`RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX`, `RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX`), GAR categorical layout migration (hallmarks/reliquaries/enshrines), payor subdir migration. Vocabulary established there is assumed background.
- BCG (`Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md`) — referenced for cross-module shellcheck discipline study.
```

Five sections of end-to-end context: why the heat exists, the geometric vs semantic split, file-level coordination, test-suite scope reservation, live-GCP scope, references back to ₣A_ for vocabulary. Approve this revision and I'll install via `jjx_paddock` setter with raised size_limit (which will also commit the staged restring).

input.content:
# jjezs_slate jjx-paddock-honor-size-limit-param

## Character

Spook capture. JJK infrastructure stumble surfaced during a real recovery scenario; small fix with a broader audit angle.

## Background

During ₣BA creation (sibling heat to ₣A_), `jjx_transfer`'s pre-commit guard blocked because the staged `jjg_gallops.json` delta exceeded the default 100KB limit. The user authorized raising the limit, and `jjx_transfer` correctly honored `size_limit: 250000` on retry — but the retry errored "Pace not found" because the first attempt had already mutated gallops.json on disk (it just couldn't commit). Recovery shifted to `jjx_paddock` (set the new heat's paddock and bundle the staged restring into one commit), but `jjx_paddock` silently ignored the same `size_limit` param and blocked at its own hardcoded ~50KB guard. Operator fell back to direct `git commit` (`0c03f527`).

## Defect

`jjx_paddock` does not accept a `size_limit` parameter. The MCP Command Reference documents the signature as `{firemark?, note?}`, and the runtime ignores any extra field. Internal guard is hardcoded at ~50000 bytes — half the default for `jjx_transfer`. The asymmetry is the bug: when paddock is the recovery vector for a transfer's blocked commit, the limit a user just authorized cannot propagate.

## Fix

1. Extend `jjx_paddock` signature to accept `size_limit?` like `jjx_record`, `jjx_close`, `jjx_archive`, `jjx_transfer`.
2. Update `Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/jjk-claude-context.md` MCP Command Reference to reflect the new param.
3. Audit other state-mutating commands for the same omission — at minimum `jjx_create`, `jjx_alter`, `jjx_relabel`, `jjx_drop`, `jjx_reorder`, `jjx_relocate`, `jjx_redocket`, `jjx_landing`. Any command that self-commits and could plausibly batch with prior staged content should honor `size_limit`. Document the audit outcome in this pace's notch.

## Verification

- Reproduce a ₣BA-like scenario (synthetic large gallops delta) and confirm `jjx_paddock {firemark, size_limit: <large>}` commits cleanly.
- CLAUDE.md doc reflects the new param.
- Audit table of other commands posted in pace notch.

## References

- Officium ☉260426-1000 session captured the original failure pattern
- Workaround commit: `0c03f527` ("jjx: restring 6 paces A_ -> BA + install BA paddock")
- Spook origin: real-time recovery during ₣BA nomination + restring
